query: does deletion of cohesin change gene expression

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: The biochemical aspects of the genetics of Turner syndrome begin early on with X inactivation. X inactivation is the process in female somatic cells where one X chromosome is inactivated or shut off, and the genes of the active X chromosome remain under transcription and those of the inactive X chromosome do not. In this process, the X-inactive specific transcript (XIST) RNA produced from the X inactivation center coats one X chromosome by random selection and utilizes methylation and modification of histone proteins to repress euchromatin and inactivate the DNA of one X chromosome [10] . However, under normal circumstances 25% of the genes on the X chromosome escape silencing many of which have homologs on the Y chromosome in men.

retrieved_docs.title: Cornelia de Lange Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple genes impacting the cohesin pathway's function have been the target of study in patients with CdLS. [1] [4] [5] The protein complex of cohesin and its regulators is essential for mitosis, as it is necessary for sister chromatid separation. [1] [5] Furthermore, the complex participates in several most biological processes involving maintenance of genome stability, regulation of gene expression, chromatin structure, and genome organization. Several genes on different chromosomes participate in the complex development, and a total of 7 genes have been identified in association with CdLS. [4] [5] Five main genes account for 70% of cases. [1] [2] Of those five genes, Nipped-B-like (NIPBL) protein on chromosome 5 accounts for approximately 60% of cases and the remainder for the remaining 10% of genetic cases. [4] [6] The other 30% of patients with CdLS are considered idiopathic. [4] Of note, the syndrome affects the sexes equally. [4] Although the disorder is genetic, almost all cases are sporadic (de novo heterozygous mutations). [1] Nevertheless, an autosomal dominant familial transmission or an x-linked dominant pattern has occasionally been observed as fewer than 1% of individuals with NIPBL-related CdLS have an affected parent.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Nondisjunction -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Mitotic nondisjunction can occur due to the inactivation of either topoisomerase II, condensin, or separase. This will result in 2 aneuploid daughter cells, one with 47 chromosomes (2n+1) and the other with 45 chromosomes (2n-1).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

Overall, cesarean delivery functions as a central node linking surgical intervention, anatomical variations (pelvic malformation, uterine binding), pregnancy outcomes (normal, abnormal, premature birth), infectious complications (endometritis), cervical cytology abnormalities, and neonatal weight concerns, thereby guiding clinicians in risk assessment and comprehensive obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2521
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are specific manifestations that fall under the broader concept of uterine function. Uterine function also includes the conditions الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation) and رحم ناقص التوتر (a uterus with reduced tone). Early placental separation is clinically associated with بضع الرحم (uterine atony), indicating that loss of uterine tone can predispose to premature detachment of the placenta. Recognizing how these forms of asynclitism, uterine functional abnormalities, and their clinical associations interrelate helps clinicians anticipate delivery complications and manage uterine tone to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Kolagen serves as the parent classification for connective tissue proteins, indicating that connective tissue proteins are members of the kolagen family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine function includes several distinct conditions that are important for obstetric care: الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), رحم ناقص التوتر (uterus with insufficient tone), and عطالة رحمية ثانوية (secondary uterine inertia). These entities represent specific abnormalities of the uterus and placenta that can affect labor progression and fetal outcomes, and they are considered sub‑categories of overall uterine function in clinical assessment and management.
